---
# Replication fixture for epy_papers. Based on a real publication used purely
# as a submission-format test target (no relationship to the venue):
#   Navarro-Mora, A. (2024). "Forensic analysis of the Upala suspension
#   bridge." IABSE Congress San Jose 2024. ISBN 9798331307189
# Targeted here against an ASCE structural journal to exercise the ascelike
# route, figures-at-end and the Practical Applications panel. Body is abridged.
title:
  en: Forensic structural analysis of the Upala pedestrian suspension bridge collapse
language: en
authors:
  - name: Angel Navarro-Mora
    orcid: 0000-0002-0539-7014
    affiliation: Escuela de Ingenieria en Construccion, Instituto Tecnologico de Costa Rica
    email: ahnavarro@itcr.ac.cr
    corresponding: true
abstract:
  en: >
    This paper presents a forensic structural analysis of the collapse of a
    pedestrian suspension bridge. Field evidence, as-built geometry and a
    nonlinear cable model are combined to reconstruct the load path and to
    identify the governing failure mechanism. The analysis indicates that the
    anchorage and the live-load assumptions were decisive, and it derives
    lessons for the assessment of similar light suspension footbridges.
keywords:
  en: [forensic engineering, suspension bridge, cable, collapse, assessment]
declarations:
  data-availability: "Field data are available from the author on request."
  ai-disclosure: "No generative AI tools were used in preparing this manuscript."
  practical-applications: >
    The findings help practitioners assess light pedestrian suspension bridges,
    where anchorage detailing and realistic live-load assumptions are critical
    to avoid the failure mode reconstructed in this study.
bibliography: navarro_upala_bridge_forensic.bib
---

# Introduction

Light pedestrian suspension bridges are common in rural networks and their
forensic assessment after failure is rarely documented [@irvine1981].

# Field evidence and as-built model

The as-built geometry and the failure surface are reconstructed from field
inspection.

# Structural analysis

A nonlinear cable model reproduces the load path up to the failure mechanism.

# Conclusions

Anchorage and live-load assumptions governed the collapse.
